''rugbyleagueproject.org''[https://www.loverugbyleague.com/stats/players/Danny-Buderus/ loverugbyleague] |updated = 14 April 2019 }} '''Danny Buderus''' (born 6 February 1978) is an Australian [[rugby league]] commentator, coach and former professional footballer who played in the 1990s, 2000s and 2010s. An [[Australia national rugby league team|Australian international]] and [[New South Wales rugby league team|New South Wales]] [[State of Origin series|State of Origin]] representative {{rlp|hk}}, he played in the [[National Rugby League]] for Australian club, the [[Newcastle Knights]], with whom he won a [[Dally M Medal]], and the [[2001 NRL Premiership]] before setting a new record for most games with the club. Buderus also played in the [[Super League]] for English club, the [[Leeds Rhinos]], with whom he won 2011's [[Super League XVI]]. He set the record for most appearances as captain of the [[New South Wales rugby league team|New South Wales]] [[State of Origin series|State of Origin]] team at 15 and for most consecutive appearances for the side at 21. Buderus also played representative football for the [[Exiles rugby league team|Exiles]] and [[Country New South Wales rugby league team|New South Wales Country]]. In 2015, he took up an interim coaching role with the Knights and continued as an assistant coach in 2016. ==Background== Buderus was born in [[Taree, New South Wales, Australia]]. He played his junior football for Taree Red Rovers and played 1 season for Taree United. In 1995, while attending [[St Francis Xavier's College, Hamilton]], Buderus played for the [[Australia national schoolboys rugby league team|Australian Schoolboys team]].{{cite web|title=SportingPulse Homepage for Australian Secondary Schools Rugby League|url=http://www.sportingpulse.com/assoc_page.cgi?c=7-2130-0-0-0&sID=26424|access-date=10 October 2008|publisher=SportingPulse|archive-date=31 January 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170131030043/http://websites.sportstg.com/assoc_page.cgi?c=7-2130-0-0-0&sID=26424|url-status=dead}} Other players to also come through this well-known rugby league nursery under the tutelage of Mark Wright include [[Owen Craigie]], [[Jarrod Mullen]], Paul Dan and [[Anthony Tupou#Background|Anthony Tupou]]. ==Professional playing career== ===Newcastle Knights=== In Round 3 of the [[1997 ARL season]] Buderus made his [[Australian Rugby League|ARL]] début for the [[Newcastle Knights]] against the [[South Queensland Crushers]]. Buderus scored his first try in first-grade in 1998 against the [[Auckland Warriors]] in Round 7 of the [[1998 NRL season]]. In 2001, Buderus was selected for [[City vs Country Origin|Country Origin]] in the annual [[City vs Country Origin]] match. In 2001, Buderus made his international début for [[Australian national rugby league team|Australia]] against [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand]]. Buderus played {{rlp|hk}} in the Knights' [[2001 NRL Grand Final]] win over the [[Parramatta Eels]], the Knights winning 30–24. Having won the [[2001 NRL Premiership]], the Knights travelled to England to play the [[2002 World Club Challenge]] against [[Super League]] champions, the [[Bradford Bulls]]. Buderus played at {{rlp|hk}}, and scored a [[Try (rugby)|try]] in Newcastle's loss. He made his [[State of Origin series|State of Origin]] début for [[New South Wales rugby league team|New South Wales]] in 2002. Buderus played his 100th [[NRL]] game in Round 14 of the [[2002 NRL season]] against the [[Parramatta Eels]]. In 2002, Buderus was named the [[Dally M]] Hooker of the Year and [[Dally M]] Representative Player of the Year at the [[Dally M Awards]] at the end of the regular season. Buderus played a pivotal role in Newcastle making the semi-finals again in 2003 and ended the season again named Hooker of the Year. In 2004, Buderus was awarded the 2004 [[Dally M Medal]] for the Player of the Year, only the second {{rlp|hk}} to be awarded the medal after [[Mal Cochrane]] as well as the 2004 Hooker of the Year. Buderus was selected in the Australian team to go and compete in the end of season [[2004 Rugby League Tri-Nations]] tournament. In the final against [[Great Britain national rugby league team|Great Britain]] he played at hooker in the Kangaroos' 44–4 victory. In 2005 Buderus again won the Hooker of the Year award. On 13 March 2008, Buderus signed a 2-year contract with the [[Leeds Rhinos]] starting in 2009.{{cite news|title=Rhinos sign Aussie star Buderus|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/super_league/leeds/7293457.stm|work=BBC News|date=13 March 2008|access-date=13 March 2008}} In August 2008, Buderus was named in the [[Australia national rugby league team#Rugby League World Cup Squad|preliminary 46-man Kangaroos squad]] for the [[2008 Rugby League World Cup]] despite comments by Kangaroos' coach, [[Ricky Stuart]], that he would not select players leaving Australia to play in England in 2009. However Buderus wasn't selected to play in a game.{{cite news|author=Liam FitzGibbon|publisher=Fox Sports News (Australia)|title=Surprises in Kangaroos squad|url=https://www.foxsports.com.au/story/0,8659,24111780-23214,00.html|access-date=2 August 2008|date=1 August 2008|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080808144730/http://www.foxsports.com.au/story/0%2C8659%2C24111780-23214%2C00.html|archive-date=8 August 2008|df=dmy}}{{cite news|url=http://www.iht.com/articles/ap/2008/08/01/sports/AS-RGL-Australia-Squad.php|title=Veteran Lockyer named in Australian squad|newspaper=[[International Herald Tribune]]|date=1 August 2008|access-date=2 August 2008}} ===Leeds Rhinos=== [[File:Buderus.PNG|thumb|left|Buderus playing for the Knights in 2012]] Buderus commenced playing in the [[Super League]] for English club the [[Leeds Rhinos]] in 2009. He re-signed with the Rhinos on a 2-year contract during 2010. He played in the [[2010 Challenge Cup Final]] defeat by the [[Warrington Wolves]] at [[Wembley Stadium]].{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/8938067.stm|title=Leeds 6–30 Warrington|publisher=BBC|date=28 August 2010|access-date=22 November 2019|first=Ged|last=Scott}}{{cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2010/aug/28/leeds-rhinos-warrington-wolves-challenge-cup-final |location=London |work=The Guardian |title=Chris Hicks hat-trick leads Warrington to Challenge Cup triumph over Leeds |first=Andy |last=Wilson |date=28 August 2010 |access-date=22 November 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100831105934/http://www.guardian.co.uk/sport/2010/aug/28/leeds-rhinos-warrington-wolves-challenge-cup-final |archive-date=31 August 2010 |url-status=live |df=dmy }} In 2011, Buderus played {{rlp|hk}} for the Leeds Rhinos in their [[2011 Challenge Cup Final]] defeat by the [[Wigan Warriors]] at [[Wembley Stadium]].{{cite news|last=Scott|first=Ged|title=Leeds 18-28 Wigan|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/14670204|access-date=21 November 2019|newspaper=[[BBC Sport]]|date=27 August 2011}}{{cite news| url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2011/aug/27/leeds-rhinos-wigan-warriors-challenge-cup | location=London | work=The Guardian | first=Andy | last=Wilson | title=Leeds Rhinos 18-28 Wigan Warriors – Challenge Cup final match report | date=27 August 2011}}{{cite news|last=Brown|first=Oliver|title=Challenge Cup final: Leeds Rhinos 18 Wigan Warriors 28|url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/sport/rugbyleague/8727501/Challenge-Cup-final-Leeds-Rhinos-18-Wigan-Warriors-28.html|access-date=21 November 2019|newspaper=The Telegraph|date=27 August 2011}} On 10 June 2011, Buderus was selected in the [[Exiles rugby league team|Exiles]] squad for the [[International Origin]] match against [[England national rugby league team|England]] at [[Headingley Rugby Stadium|Headingley]] while playing for the [[Leeds Rhinos]] of the [[Super League]]. He also captained the side to a victory.{{cite web|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/13278410|title=Brian McClennan completes Exiles squad to face England fixture|work=BBC Sport|date=4 May 2011|archive-date=13 January 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160113141331/https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/13278410|url-status=live}}{{Cite news |date=2011-06-08 |title=George Carmont snatches Exiles victory at Headingley |url=https://www.bbc.com/sport/rugby-league/13696752 |access-date=2024-04-22 |work=BBC Sport |language=en-GB}} In 2011, Buderus played {{rlp|hk}} for Leeds in their [[2011 Super League Grand Final]] win over [[St Helens R.F.C.|St Helens]], with Leeds winning 30–16 at [[Old Trafford]], in which was his final game for the club.{{cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2011/oct/08/super-league-grand-final-match-report?newsfeed=true|title=Leeds claim Grand Final glory as inspired Rob Burrow sinks St Helens|date=8 October 2011|work=Guardian|access-date=21 November 2019}}{{cite web|url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/sport/rugbyleague/8815944/St-Helens-16-Leeds-32-match-report.html|title=St Helens 16 Leeds 32|date=8 October 2011|work=Daily Telegraph|access-date=21 November 2019}} After new [[Newcastle Knights]] coach [[Wayne Bennett (rugby league)|Wayne Bennett]] requested Buderus' services, Buderus signed a 1-year contract on 1 October 2011 with the [[Newcastle Knights]] starting in 2012, returning to the club he had played 220 games for. He had been released from the final year of his [[Leeds Rhinos]] contract to sign with Newcastle. ===Return to Newcastle=== In 2012, 7 weeks into his return to the [[National Rugby League]], Buderus was again selected for [[City vs Country Origin|Country Origin]] at starting {{rlp|hk}},http://www.newcastleknights.com.au/newsdisplay/Four-Knights-players-selected-in-Country-side/53360{{dead link|date=January 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes}} however he withdrew due to injury. Later in the year there was speculation he would be named at {{rlp|hk}} for [[New South Wales rugby league team|New South Wales]] in Game 1 of the [[2012 State of Origin series]] but was injured and didn't get picked, [[Robbie Farah]] was instead picked and retained his spot for the rest of the series. On 12 June 2012, Buderus re-signed with the Knights on a 1-year contract, 14 rounds into the [[2012 NRL season|2012 season]].{{cite web|url=http://www.newcastleknights.com.au/news-display/Buderus-resigns-for-2013/56963|title=Buderus re-signs for 2013 - Newcastle Knights|access-date=2012-11-18|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131019122751/http://www.newcastleknights.com.au/news-display/Buderus-resigns-for-2013/56963|archive-date=19 October 2013|df=dmy}} In September 2013, Buderus announced his retirement at the end of the [[2013 NRL season]]. During the Knights vs Roosters Preliminary Final, Buderus entered a tackle and made contact with the elbow of [[Jared Waerea-Hargreaves]], knocking him out. This saw Buderus stretchered from the field, ending his career. However, later scans in hospital cleared the retiring hooker of any serious head or neck injuries.{{Cite web|url=https://www.foxsports.com.au/nrl/nrl-premiership/danny-buderus-cleared-of-serious-injury-after-being-knocked-out-in-nrl-preliminary-final/story-fn2mcuj6-1226729419888|title=Motorsport Video |Motorsport Highlights, Replays, News, Clips}} == Accolades == === Individual === * [[Dally M Awards|Dally M]] Player of the Year: 2004 * 3x [[Dally M Awards|Dally M]] Hooker of the Year: 2002, 2004, 2005 * [[Dally M Awards|Dally M]] Representative Player of the Year: 2002 * RLPA Player of the Year: 2004 * [[Brad Fittler Medal]]: 2009 *[[National Rugby League]] Hall of Fame inductee: #110 2019 == Coaching career == Buderus stayed with the Knights as an assistant coach for 2014. In July 2015, Buderus was made interim head coach, following the sacking of [[Rick Stone]].{{cite news| url=https://www.theage.com.au/rugby-league/newcastle-knights/newcastle-knights-sack-coach-rick-stone-with-danny-buderus-his-replacement-20150727-gild2g.html| title=Newcastle Knights sack coach Rick Stone with Danny Buderus his replacement| newspaper=theage.com.au| date=27 July 2015| access-date=27 July 2015}} After the 2016 season, he chose to leave his role as an assistant coach. He served as pathways coach in 2017. Buderus was also a selector{{when|date=May 2023}} for the NSW Blues helping select the series winning side along with [[Greg Alexander]] and former Knights teammate and [[Rugby League]] [[The Immortals (rugby league)|Immortal]] [[Andrew Johns]]. On 28 September 2023, Buderus was informed by the NSWRL board that his contract would not be renewed for the following season after New South Wales suffered another series loss.{{cite web|url=https://www.nrl.com/news/2023/09/28/fittler-quits-nsw-on-lookout-for-new-origin-coach/|title=Fittler quits: NSW on lookout for new Origin coach|website=www.nrl.com|date=28 September 2023 }} On 15 October 2025, the Knights confirmed Buderus had re-joined the Knights in an assistant coaching role.{{Cite web |date=2025-10-15 |title=Knights appoint NRL Coaches for 2026 |url=https://www.newcastleknights.com.au/news/2025/10/15/knights-appoint-nrl-coaches-for-2026/ |access-date=2025-10-15 |website=Newcastle Knights |language=en}} ==Personal life== Buderus is founder and co-owner of Steel City Beer Company with [[Andrew Johns]], [[Matthew Johns]], [[Kurt Gidley]] and [[Matt Hoy]].{{cite web | url=https://pubtic.com.au/newcastle-legends-behind-launch-of-steel-city-beer-co/#:~:text=Steel%20City%27s%20founders%20include%20proud,and%20Sophie%20and%20Ryan%20Ginns | title=Newcastle Legends Behind Launch of Steel City Beer Co | date=16 April 2021 }}{{cite web | url=https://www.newcastleherald.com.au/story/7663201/knights-say-cheers-to-stadium-beers/ | title=Knights say cheers to stadium beers | date=18 March 2022 }} ==References== {{Reflist|2}} ==Sources==
